items.q: Why does a company's share price change?
items.correct: Share prices reflect investors' expectations about future profits — driven by company performance, sector trends, economic conditions, and market sentiment.
items.misconception: Share prices go up when a company makes more money this year.
items.random_wrong: Share prices are set by the government each morning.
items.why: Markets are forward-looking. A company can report record profits and see shares fall if results were worse than expected. Students miss the expectations-driven nature of pricing.

items.q: What is corporate governance, and how does it differ from CSR?
items.correct: Corporate governance is the system of rules, practices and processes by which a company is directed and controlled — focusing on accountability, board structure and shareholder rights. CSR is about wider social/environmental responsibility.
items.misconception: Corporate governance is the same as corporate social responsibility.
items.random_wrong: Corporate governance is the legal requirement to have a CEO.
items.why: Governance is about how the company is run (board, audit, transparency); CSR is about what the company does for society. Students conflate them — both concern ethics but at different levels.

items.q: What is Herzberg's distinction between hygiene factors and motivators?
items.correct: Hygiene factors (pay, conditions, policies) prevent dissatisfaction when present but don't motivate; motivators (recognition, achievement, responsibility) create positive satisfaction.
items.random_wrong: Hygiene factors are about workplace cleanliness.
items.why: Pay is a hygiene factor — its absence demotivates but its presence doesn't motivate beyond a point. Students treat pay as the prime motivator; Herzberg's theory directly challenges this.

items.q: A firm with only cash cows risks:
items.correct: Excess liquidity now but no future
items.why: Correct answer: Excess liquidity now but no future. “Bankruptcy this year” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Stars. “Dogs” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Stages a successful product passes through: QM, Star, Cow (count)?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
